figures.gloss: An illiterate, destitute widow of Ayabe (1837–1918) who at fifty-five was violently possessed by the kami Ushitora no Konjin and, though she had never learned to write, began producing automatic script in phonetic kana — a torrent of oracles continuing until her death. Her message was a peasant woman's judgment on Meiji modernity: the world of the strong devouring the weak must be torn down and rebuilt.
figures.facet: The starkest case of the Japanese new-religion founder pattern — divine authority erupting at the absolute bottom of the social order, in a woman with no literacy, property, or standing, whose possession itself became the credential.
figures.provenance: Deguchi Nao, Ofudesaki (1892–1918); Yasumaru Yoshio, Deguchi Nao (1977); Emily Groszos Ooms, Women and Millenarian Protest in Meiji Japan: Deguchi Nao and Ōmotokyō (1993).

figures.gloss: Born Ueda Kisaburō (1871–1948); a spirit-medium trained on Mount Takakuma who married Nao's daughter Sumiko, took the Deguchi name, and built Nao's oracles into a vast movement. He dictated the 81-volume Reikai Monogatari from his spirit-world journeys, adopted Esperanto (1923) as the tongue of coming world unity, rode into Mongolia in 1924 declaring a mission to unite Asia's faiths, and taught bankyō dōkon — that all religions grow from one root. Twice arrested in the state's suppressions of 1921 and 1935.
figures.facet: The flamboyant showman-prophet as organizational genius: Onisaburō posed as Miroku (Maitreya), dressed as goddesses for the camera, and courted mass media decades before it was ordinary — charisma run as deliberate publicity, which is precisely why the imperial state judged him a rival sovereignty and crushed the movement twice.
figures.provenance: Deguchi Onisaburō, Reikai Monogatari (dictated 1921–1934); Nancy K. Stalker, Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan (2008).

figures.gloss: The feared 'golden kami of the northeast' — folk religion's most dangerous directional spirit — revealed through Nao as the true primal creator (identified with Kunitokotachi of the classical chronicles), unjustly banished by usurping kami to the demon-gate direction and now returning to judge and remake the world.
figures.facet: A deliberate inversion of folk demonology: the most avoided spirit in the calendar-diviner's repertoire is unmasked as the wronged original god, turning popular fear itself into evidence of a primordial injustice awaiting redress.

figures.gloss: The 'tip of the brush': Nao's automatic script, produced in trance from 1892 until her death in 1918 and traditionally reckoned at some 200,000 pages of phonetic kana. Its refrain announces tatekae tatenaoshi — the tearing down and rebuilding of the world — and the dawn of the divine age when 'the greater world shall burst into bloom as plum blossoms at winter's end.'
figures.facet: Scripture whose authority rests on the writer's incapacity — an unlettered hand producing text was itself the proof that the kami, not the woman, was writing.
figures.provenance: Deguchi Nao, Ofudesaki (1892–1918; selections in Ōmoto Shin'yu); Yasumaru (1977); Ooms (1993).

figures.gloss: The core millenarian teaching: the present order — greedy, militarist, upside-down — must be demolished and reconstructed as the divine world of Miroku. Not reform but total renovation (yonaoshi radicalized), with the poor and despised vindicated in the rebuilt world.
figures.facet: Peasant world-renewal eschatology given a permanent institutional home: the demolition-and-reconstruction of the cosmos doubled as a barely-coded verdict on the Meiji state, which is why Oomoto alone among the new religions drew two full-scale suppressions (1921, 1935).

figures.gloss: The technique Onisaburō systematized in the 1910s: a seated practitioner is brought into controlled possession while a mediator (saniwa) interrogates and identifies the possessing spirit. It drew thousands to Ayabe around 1916–1921 before being curtailed after the first suppression.
figures.facet: The practice made Oomoto the seedbed of a whole lineage of light-and-spirit techniques: Sekai Kyūsei Kyō's jōrei and Mahikari's okiyome descend from this milieu, and Ueshiba Morihei — Onisaburō's devotee from 1920 — carried its vocabulary of spirit-pacification into aikido.
figures.provenance: Birgit Staemmler, Chinkon Kishin: Mediated Spirit Possession in Japanese New Religions (2009); Stalker (2008).

figures.gloss: 'Tales of the Spirit World': 81 volumes Deguchi Onisaburō dictated between 1921 and 1934 (the work interrupted by his 1921 arrest and imprisonment), recounting his own journeys through the spirit worlds and laying out a systematic cosmology — Oomoto's second scripture alongside Nao's Ofudesaki.
figures.facet: Where Nao's Ofudesaki is involuntary trance-writing from an illiterate hand, Reikai Monogatari is a deliberate, first-person travelogue by a trained spirit-medium — the same movement's two scriptures embody its two founders' opposite relationships to revelation, one seized by it and one touring it.
figures.provenance: Deguchi Onisaburō, Reikai Monogatari (dictated 1921–1934); Nancy K. Stalker, 'Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan' (2008).

figures.gloss: Nao's youngest daughter (1883–1952), who married Onisaburō — bringing him into the Deguchi name and family leadership — and became Oomoto's Second Spiritual Leader (Kyōshu) after Nao's death in 1918, holding the organization together through the 1921 and 1935 state suppressions and its postwar rebuilding.
figures.facet: She is the hinge of Oomoto's distinctive female-line succession: the office of spiritual leader has passed through Deguchi women ever since, even though Onisaburō supplied the movement's public cosmology, organization, and reach — authority and administration run on two separate, gendered tracks in this tradition.
figures.provenance: Nancy K. Stalker, 'Prophet Motive' (2008); Emily Groszos Ooms, 'Women and Millenarian Protest in Meiji Japan: Deguchi Nao and Ōmotokyō' (1993).

figures.gloss: Onisaburō's doctrine that every religion grows from a single divine source, put into institutional practice through Oomoto's interfaith alliances with other new religions and its adoption of Esperanto (from 1923) as the language of a coming, religiously unified world.
figures.facet: It converts interfaith outreach from tactic into doctrine: Oomoto's alliances and its Esperanto campaign were not diplomatic convenience but direct enactments of a stated theological claim, which is part of why the imperial state read the movement as a rival universal authority rather than a merely local cult.
figures.provenance: Nancy K. Stalker, 'Prophet Motive' (2008), discussing bankyō dōkon and its institutional expressions.

edges.note: Oomoto exalts the banished primal kami (identified with Kunitokotachi of the Shinto chronicles) above the Amaterasu-centered imperial orthodoxy — a documented doctrinal inversion of State Shinto that the state prosecuted as lèse-majesté in 1921 and 1935.

Two peasant women seized by a kami revealed as humanity's true parent, each producing automatic scripture called Ofudesaki and founding a world-renewal religion — the same founder pattern, one generation apart, with no proven borrowing.
World-renewal eschatology in two East Asian new religions: the demolition-and-rebuilding of the world and the Great Opening both announce a total cosmic reconstruction vindicating the downtrodden.
Revelation by automatic writing: the entranced hand as the deity's instrument, in Nao's kana torrent and the beaked basket of the Cao Đài séances.
Spirit-writing as the engine of scripture — fuji planchette revelation and Nao's trance script both ground a new canon in writing produced past the writer's own hand.
Intra-tradition: the raw oracle and her systematizer — Nao's possession supplied the revelation, Onisaburō the cosmology, organization, and reach; the tradition is their fusion.

Documented lineage: Deguchi Nao encountered Konjin faith through Konkokyo, and her early group operated under Konkokyo affiliation (c. 1893–1897) before Oomoto's independence — the fountainhead religion's own fountainhead.

The same folk deity rehabilitated twice in one lineage: the feared Konjin becomes Konkokyo's parent of the universe, and via Nao's Konkokyo-mediated encounter becomes Oomoto's banished creator — attested descent, differently theologized.

Documented lineage with one intermediate step: Okada Kōtama was a Sekai Kyūsei Kyō member, whose founder Okada Mokichi developed jōrei light-channeling after his years as an Oomoto leader — okiyome descends from the Oomoto spirit-pacification milieu through that chain.

The one-root-of-all-religions claim: Mahikari's teaching that all faiths refract Su God's single light recognizably continues the bankyō dōkon universalism Onisaburō preached — a lineage-plausible echo, tiered as analogy since direct doctrinal transmission is not documented.

Two Bakumatsu/Meiji-era Japanese new-religion canons formed from a founder's direct output — Konko Daijin's retrospective memoir and recorded oral teachings beside Deguchi Nao's automatic writings — parallel modes of scripture-formation in the same religious wave, without one deriving from the other.
Intra-tradition: Onisaburō's own dictated scripture, the direct textual record of his spirit-world journeys.
Intra-tradition: the movement's two co-canonical scriptures, one per founder — Nao's automatic kana script and Onisaburō's dictated spirit-travelogue.
Intra-tradition: mother and daughter — Nao the First Spiritual Leader, Sumiko the Second, the founding link in Oomoto's female-line succession.
Intra-tradition: husband and wife, and co-leaders of the movement through its two state suppressions.
Intra-tradition: Onisaburō is bankyō dōkon's author and its chief institutional promoter, via Oomoto's interfaith alliances and Esperanto adoption.

The one-root-of-all-religions claim now anchored to its own dedicated figure: Mahikari's teaching that all faiths refract Su God's single light recognizably continues the universalism Onisaburō preached as bankyō dōkon — lineage-plausible echo, tiered as analogy since direct doctrinal transmission is not documented.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both hold that the world's religions share one divine source, though Bahá'í stages that source as a sequence of historical Manifestations while Oomoto holds all roots as simultaneously present and equally valid — independently arrived-at universalisms, not shared descent.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same term 'Ofudesaki' independently applied to the automatic-writing scripture of two 19th-century Japanese peasant founders each revealed as a channel of humanity's true parent — a shared genre of Japanese new-religion revelation, not demonstrated textual borrowing.

dossier.essence: A Japanese new religion born in Ayabe in 1892, when the illiterate peasant widow Deguchi Nao (1837-1918) was possessed by the kami Ushitora no Konjin and began producing automatic script announcing the demolition and rebuilding of the world. Organized and vastly expanded by her son-in-law Deguchi Onisaburō (1871-1948), Oomoto ('Great Origin') became one of imperial Japan's largest and most provocative religious movements — crushed by the state in 1921 and again in 1935 — and survives today as a modest body headquartered at Ayabe and Kameoka, known for its teaching that all religions grow from one root (bankyō dōkon) and its championing of Esperanto.
dossier.cosmos: The true primal creator — the feared folk demon Ushitora no Konjin, unmasked as Kunitokotachi of the classical chronicles — was banished to the northeast 'demon gate' by usurping kami, and his return signals tatekae tatenaoshi: the tearing down and rebuilding of the present greedy, inverted order into the divine world of Miroku. Onisaburō's Reikai Monogatari maps an elaborate spirit world, traversed in his own visionary journeys, in which the spirit continues after death; the human being is understood as a vessel through which kami act. Suffering and injustice are read as symptoms of the primordial usurpation awaiting cosmic redress.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on the two sacred grounds — Baishōen at Ayabe and Ten'onkyō at Kameoka — with Shinto-form liturgy, monthly festivals, and ancestral rites. The controlled-possession technique chinkon kishin drew thousands around 1916-1921 before being curtailed after the first suppression; the movement instead cultivated the arts (calligraphy, ceramics, tea) as spiritual practice, promoted Esperanto as the tongue of coming unity, and has run interfaith and humanitarian work through the Jinrui Aizenkai (Universal Love and Brotherhood Association, founded 1925).
dossier.canon: Nao's Ofudesaki — trance-written kana script produced 1892-1918, traditionally reckoned at some 200,000 pages, with selections circulating as the Ōmoto Shin'yu — and Onisaburō's 81-volume Reikai Monogatari (dictated 1921-1934).
dossier.reflects: Millenarian inversion — the impulse to crown the lowest of society with divine authority and read the reigning order as a usurpation scheduled for demolition.
sources: Emily Groszos Ooms, 'Women and Millenarian Protest in Meiji Japan: Deguchi Nao and Ōmotokyō' (1993)
Nancy K. Stalker, 'Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan' (2007)
Birgit Staemmler, 'Chinkon Kishin: Mediated Spirit Possession in Japanese New Religions' (2009)

links.cite: Emily Groszos Ooms, 'Women and Millenarian Protest in Meiji Japan: Deguchi Nao and Ōmotokyō' (1993)
Nancy K. Stalker, 'Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan' (2007)
